terms.definition: Existing in three dimensions — length, width, and height — occupying physical space.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Calculate the volume of this 3D shape.

terms.definition: Applying trigonometric ratios to problems involving three-dimensional shapes, often by identifying right-angled triangles within the solid.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use 3D trigonometry to find the angle between a diagonal and the base of a cuboid.
terms.common_errors: Not identifying the correct right-angled triangle embedded in the 3D shape.

terms.definition: A probability rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B), used for finding the probability of either event occurring.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the addition law to find the probability of drawing a red card or a king.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to subtract the overlap P(A and B) for non-mutually exclusive events.

terms.definition: A fraction where the numerator, denominator, or both contain algebraic expressions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Simplify the algebraic fraction (x² - 4)/(x + 2).
terms.common_errors: Cancelling individual terms instead of common factors.

terms.definition: A circle theorem stating that the angle between a tangent and a chord equals the angle in the alternate segment.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the alternate segment theorem to find the missing angle.
terms.common_errors: Confusing which angle is in the alternate segment.

terms.definition: A situation in trigonometry where the sine rule produces two possible solutions for an angle or triangle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Check for the ambiguous case when using the sine rule — there may be two valid triangles.
terms.common_errors: Not recognising that sin(θ) = sin(180° - θ) gives a second possible angle.

terms.definition: The maximum displacement from the centre line of a periodic function; half the distance between the peak and trough.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The amplitude of y = 3sin(x) is 3.
terms.common_errors: Confusing amplitude with period.

terms.definition: A circle theorem: the angle at the centre of a circle is twice the angle at the circumference when both subtend the same arc.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The angle at the centre is 120°, so the angle at the circumference is 60°.
terms.common_errors: Applying the theorem when the angles don't subtend the same arc.

terms.definition: An angle formed at a point on the circumference of a circle by two chords meeting at that point.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc.

terms.definition: The angle measured downward from the horizontal to a line of sight to an object below.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: From the top of a cliff, the angle of depression to a boat is 35°.
terms.common_errors: Confusing angle of depression (looking down) with angle of elevation (looking up).

terms.definition: The angle measured upward from the horizontal to a line of sight to an object above.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The angle of elevation from the ground to the top of the tower is 52°.
terms.common_errors: Measuring from the vertical instead of the horizontal.

terms.definition: A mathematical rule for calculating the area of a specific shape (e.g. A = ½absinC for a triangle).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the area formula A = ½absinC to find the area of this non-right-angled triangle.
terms.common_errors: Using the wrong formula for the given shape or information.

terms.definition: A type of sequence where consecutive terms have a constant difference.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3, 7, 11, 15 is an arithmetic sequence with common difference 4.
terms.common_errors: Confusing arithmetic (constant difference) with geometric (constant ratio) sequences.

terms.definition: A relationship or connection between two variables shown in statistical data.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The scatter graph shows a positive association between height and shoe size.
terms.common_errors: Assuming association implies causation.

terms.definition: Relating to Bayes' theorem, which calculates conditional probability using prior probabilities and new evidence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: Named after Thomas Bayes (1701-1761), English mathematician
terms.example_usage: Use Bayes' theorem to find the probability of having a condition given a positive test result.
terms.common_errors: Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A).

terms.definition: A direction measured clockwise from north, given as a three-digit angle (e.g. 045°, 270°).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The bearing from A to B is 135°.
terms.common_errors: Not measuring from north, or measuring anticlockwise.

terms.definition: A systematic error in data collection that makes results unrepresentative of the population.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Surveying only morning shoppers introduces bias into the sample.

terms.definition: Involving two variables; bivariate data has paired values for two different measurements.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Plot this bivariate data on a scatter graph.

terms.definition: A diagram showing the distribution of data using the minimum, lower quartile, median, upper quartile, and maximum.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a box plot to compare the test scores of two classes.
terms.common_errors: Not drawing the box from Q1 to Q3 with the median line inside.

terms.definition: A direct cause-and-effect relationship where one variable actually produces a change in another.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Correlation does not imply causation — ice cream sales and drowning rates both rise in summer, but one doesn't cause the other.
terms.common_errors: Confusing correlation (association) with causation (cause-and-effect).

terms.definition: A survey that collects data from every member of a population, not just a sample.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A census of the school would ask every student, not just a sample.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a census (everyone) with a sample (a subset).

terms.definition: Points that all lie on the same straight line.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Show that points A(1,2), B(3,4), and C(5,6) are collinear.

terms.definition: A vector written vertically as a column with the horizontal component on top and vertical component below.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The translation is described by the column vector (3, -2).
terms.common_errors: Confusing the order — top number is horizontal (x), bottom is vertical (y).

terms.definition: A method for rewriting a quadratic expression in the form (x + p)² + q, useful for finding the vertex and solving equations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Complete the square: x² + 6x + 5 = (x + 3)² - 4.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to subtract the square of half the coefficient.

terms.definition: Made up of two or more combined parts; in maths, can refer to composite shapes, numbers, or functions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the area of this composite shape by splitting it into rectangles and triangles.

terms.definition: A function created by applying one function to the result of another, written as fg(x) or f(g(x)).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If f(x) = 2x and g(x) = x + 3, then fg(x) = 2(x + 3) = 2x + 6.
terms.common_errors: Applying functions in the wrong order — fg(x) means apply g first, then f.

terms.definition: A 3D shape formed by combining two or more basic solids (e.g. a cylinder topped with a hemisphere).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the volume of this composite solid made from a cone on top of a cylinder.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to add (or subtract) the volumes of the component shapes.

terms.definition: Interest calculated on both the original amount and any previously accumulated interest, growing exponentially.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: After 3 years at 5% compound interest, £1000 becomes £1000 × 1.05³ = £1157.63.
terms.common_errors: Using simple interest calculation instead of the compound formula.

terms.definition: The probability of an event occurring given that another event has already happened, written as P(A|B).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: P(rain|cloudy) is the probability of rain given that it is cloudy.
terms.common_errors: Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A) — they are generally different.

terms.definition: Rules for proving two triangles are congruent: SSS, SAS, ASA, and RHS.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the SAS congruence criterion to prove these triangles are identical.
terms.common_errors: Using AAA — equal angles don't guarantee equal sides (that's similarity).

terms.definition: A pair of expressions that are the same except for the sign between the terms (e.g. a + √b and a - √b).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Multiply by the conjugate to rationalise the denominator.

terms.definition: The reverse statement of a theorem — if the original says 'if A then B', the converse says 'if B then A'.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The converse of Pythagoras' theorem: if a² + b² = c², the triangle is right-angled.
terms.common_errors: Assuming the converse is automatically true — it must be proved separately.

terms.definition: A formula relating the sides and angles of any triangle: a² = b² + c² - 2bc·cos(A), used when you know two sides and the included angle or all three sides.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the cosine rule to find the missing side when you know two sides and the included angle.
terms.common_errors: Using the cosine rule when the sine rule would be simpler.

terms.definition: A polynomial of degree 3, with the highest power of x being x³; the graph has up to two turning points.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Sketch the cubic graph y = x³ - 3x.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the shape of a cubic graph with a quadratic.

terms.definition: A running total of frequencies, showing how many data values fall at or below each class boundary.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Plot a cumulative frequency graph to estimate the median.
terms.common_errors: Plotting at the midpoint instead of the upper class boundary.

terms.definition: A quadrilateral whose four vertices all lie on the circumference of a circle; opposite angles sum to 180°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles add up to 180°.
terms.common_errors: Not checking that all four vertices lie on the circle.

terms.definition: The process of gathering information in a structured way for statistical analysis.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Design a data collection sheet to record eye colours.

terms.definition: The number of digits shown after the decimal point, indicating the precision of a value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Give your answer to 2 decimal places: 3.14.
terms.common_errors: Confusing decimal places with significant figures.

terms.definition: Events where the outcome of one affects the probability of the other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Drawing two cards without replacement — the events are dependent.
terms.common_errors: Treating dependent events as independent when calculating probabilities.

terms.definition: An algebraic identity: a² - b² = (a + b)(a - b), used for factorising expressions with two squared terms.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Factorise x² - 25 using the difference of two squares: (x + 5)(x - 5).
terms.common_errors: Trying to use this with a sum of squares (a² + b²), which doesn't factorise over the reals.

terms.definition: The expression b² - 4ac from the quadratic formula, which determines the number of real roots.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If the discriminant is positive, the quadratic has two distinct real roots.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting the minus sign — it's b² minus 4ac.

terms.definition: The set of all possible input values (x-values) for a function.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The domain of f(x) = 1/x is all real numbers except 0.
terms.common_errors: Confusing domain (inputs) with range (outputs).

terms.definition: A method for solving simultaneous equations by adding or subtracting equations to remove one variable.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: From Latin 'eliminare' (to put out of doors)
terms.example_usage: Use elimination: add the equations 2x + y = 7 and 3x - y = 8 to eliminate y.
terms.common_errors: Not multiplying equations first to make coefficients match.

terms.definition: An estimate of probability based on the results of an actual experiment or trial, rather than theory.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: After 100 coin flips with 47 heads, the experimental probability of heads is 0.47.
terms.common_errors: Expecting experimental probability to exactly match theoretical probability.

terms.definition: A formula that directly calculates the nth term of a sequence from n, without needing previous terms.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The explicit formula for 3, 5, 7, 9... is 2n + 1.
terms.common_errors: Confusing explicit formulas (direct calculation) with recursive formulas (using previous terms).

terms.definition: Growth where a quantity increases by a constant percentage or multiplier in each time period, producing rapid acceleration.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A population increasing by 5% each year shows exponential growth.
terms.common_errors: Confusing exponential growth with linear growth.

terms.definition: Estimating a value outside the range of known data by extending a trend, which is less reliable than interpolation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Extrapolating beyond the data range is unreliable — the trend may not continue.
terms.common_errors: Assuming the trend continues indefinitely.

terms.definition: The process of writing an expression as a product of its factors, the reverse of expanding.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Factorise x² + 5x + 6 = (x + 2)(x + 3).
terms.common_errors: Getting the signs wrong when factorising quadratics.

terms.definition: In compound measures, the push or pull acting on an object, measured in newtons (N); used in pressure = force ÷ area.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If the force is 200 N and the area is 4 m², the pressure is 50 N/m².

terms.definition: An exponent written as a fraction, where the denominator indicates a root and the numerator a power.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 8^(2/3) means the cube root of 8 squared: ³√8 = 2, then 2² = 4.
terms.common_errors: Not knowing whether to root first or power first — either order works.

terms.definition: A line graph drawn by connecting the midpoints of the tops of bars in a frequency diagram.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a frequency polygon to compare two data distributions on the same axes.
terms.common_errors: Plotting at class boundaries instead of midpoints.

terms.definition: The solid that remains when the top of a cone or pyramid is cut off by a plane parallel to the base.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the volume of the frustum by subtracting the small cone from the large cone.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to find the dimensions of the removed cone using similar triangles.

terms.definition: The unit grams per cubic centimetre, used to measure density.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The density of water is 1 g/cm³.
terms.common_errors: Confusing density units with volume units.

terms.definition: A type of sequence where consecutive terms have a constant ratio (multiplier).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 2, 6, 18, 54 is a geometric sequence with common ratio 3.
terms.common_errors: Confusing geometric (constant ratio) with arithmetic (constant difference).

terms.definition: A phrase indicating a condition has already occurred, used in conditional probability: P(A given that B).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the probability of drawing a king, given that the card is red.
terms.common_errors: Ignoring the condition and using unconditional probability.

terms.definition: Half of a sphere, cut along a great circle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The volume of a hemisphere is (2/3)πr³.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to halve the sphere formula.

terms.definition: The angle between two known sides of a triangle, needed for the sine area formula or cosine rule.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: To use A = ½absinC, you need two sides and the included angle between them.
terms.common_errors: Using an angle that is not between the two known sides.

terms.definition: Events where the outcome of one does not affect the probability of the other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rolling a dice and flipping a coin are independent events.
terms.common_errors: Treating dependent events (like drawing without replacement) as independent.

terms.definition: The point where a line or curve crosses an axis; the y-intercept is where x = 0.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The y-intercept of y = 3x + 2 is (0, 2).
terms.common_errors: Confusing the y-intercept (constant term) with the gradient (coefficient of x).

terms.definition: Estimating a value within the range of known data, which is more reliable than extrapolation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use interpolation to estimate the median from a cumulative frequency graph.
terms.common_errors: Confusing interpolation (within data range) with extrapolation (outside data range).

terms.definition: A property or point that does not change under a transformation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Under a reflection in the y-axis, points on the y-axis are invariant.

terms.definition: A function that reverses the effect of the original function; written as f⁻¹(x).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If f(x) = 2x + 3, then f⁻¹(x) = (x - 3)/2.
terms.common_errors: Confusing f⁻¹(x) (inverse function) with 1/f(x) (reciprocal).

terms.definition: A transformation that returns a shape to its original position after a given transformation has been applied.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The inverse of a reflection in the x-axis is another reflection in the x-axis.

terms.definition: Functions that find an angle from a trigonometric ratio: sin⁻¹, cos⁻¹, tan⁻¹ (also written arcsin, arccos, arctan).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If sin(x) = 0.5, then x = sin⁻¹(0.5) = 30°.
terms.common_errors: Confusing sin⁻¹(x) with 1/sin(x) — they are completely different.

terms.definition: A number that cannot be written as a fraction of two integers; its decimal form is non-terminating and non-repeating.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: √2 and π are irrational numbers.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all square roots are irrational — √4 = 2 is rational.

terms.definition: A constant of proportionality in direct or inverse proportion problems.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If y is directly proportional to x, then y = kx where k is a constant.

terms.definition: Kilometres per hour; a compound unit of speed measuring distance in km divided by time in hours.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The car travels at 60 km/h.
terms.common_errors: Confusing km/h with m/s when converting between units.

terms.definition: Writing out all possible outcomes systematically, especially in probability and combinatorics.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use listing to find all factor pairs of 24.
terms.common_errors: Missing outcomes through unsystematic listing.

terms.definition: Metres per second; the SI unit of speed.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The runner's speed is 8 m/s.

terms.definition: A rule that assigns each input value to exactly one output value; another word for function.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The mapping x → 2x + 1 sends 3 to 7.

terms.definition: A single value representing the centre or typical value of a data set: mean, median, or mode.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The mean, median, and mode are all measures of central tendency.
terms.common_errors: Always using the mean when the median might be more appropriate (e.g. with outliers).

terms.definition: A triangle that does not contain a 90° angle, requiring the sine rule or cosine rule to solve.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the sine rule to find a missing side in this non-right-angled triangle.
terms.common_errors: Attempting to use SOH-CAH-TOA, which only works for right-angled triangles.

terms.definition: Function notation where f(x) represents the output of function f when the input is x.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.common_errors: Interpreting f(x) as f multiplied by x.

terms.definition: Another term for a non-right-angled triangle — a triangle with no 90° angle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the cosine rule for this oblique triangle.

terms.definition: A function where each input has a unique output and each output comes from a unique input; necessary for an inverse to exist.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: f(x) = 2x + 1 is one-to-one, so its inverse exists.

terms.definition: Notation for conditional probability: the probability of event B occurring given that event A has already occurred.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: P(B|A) = P(A and B) / P(A).
terms.common_errors: Confusing P(B|A) with P(A|B).

terms.definition: The horizontal length of one complete cycle of a periodic function like sine or cosine.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The period of y = sin(2x) is 180° (or π radians).
terms.common_errors: Confusing period with amplitude.

terms.definition: The entire group about which statistical conclusions are to be drawn.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The population is all Year 11 students; the sample is 50 of them.
terms.common_errors: Confusing population with sample.

terms.definition: A vector from the origin to a specific point, describing that point's location.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The position vector of point A(3, 5) is (3, 5).
terms.common_errors: Confusing position vectors with displacement vectors between two points.

terms.definition: An updated probability calculated after taking new evidence into account, typically using Bayes' theorem.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: After the test result, the posterior probability of having the condition changes.

terms.definition: A compound measure: force per unit area, calculated as pressure = force ÷ area.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If 300 N acts on 6 m², the pressure is 50 N/m².
terms.common_errors: Confusing the formula — pressure = force/area, not force × area.

terms.definition: Having exactly two factors: 1 and itself. At KS4, also used for derivative notation (f'(x)).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Express 360 as a product of prime factors.
terms.common_errors: Including 1 as prime.

terms.definition: A relationship where two quantities change at a constant rate; if one doubles, the other doubles (direct) or halves (inverse).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: y is proportional to x means y = kx for some constant k.
terms.common_errors: Confusing direct proportion (y = kx) with inverse proportion (y = k/x).

terms.definition: The formula x = (-b ± √(b²-4ac)) / 2a for solving any quadratic equation ax² + bx + c = 0.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the quadratic formula to solve 2x² + 3x - 5 = 0.
terms.common_errors: Sign errors, especially with the -b or the ±.

terms.definition: A structured set of questions used to collect data from respondents.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Design a questionnaire to find out how students travel to school.
terms.common_errors: Writing leading or biased questions.

terms.definition: A sample where every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Select a random sample of 30 students using a random number generator.
terms.common_errors: Thinking convenience sampling (asking friends) is random.

terms.definition: A number that can be expressed as a fraction p/q where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 0.75 is rational because it equals 3/4.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all decimals are irrational — terminating and recurring decimals are rational.

terms.definition: To eliminate a surd from the denominator of a fraction by multiplying top and bottom by a suitable expression.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rationalise 1/√3: multiply by √3/√3 to get √3/3.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to multiply both numerator and denominator.

terms.definition: Solutions to an equation that are real numbers (not complex); where a graph crosses the x-axis.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: x² - 4 = 0 has two real roots: x = 2 and x = -2.
terms.common_errors: Confusing 'no real roots' with 'no solutions' — there may be complex roots.

terms.definition: A decimal with one or more digits that repeat infinitely in a pattern.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 1/3 = 0.333... is a recurring decimal.
terms.common_errors: Not placing dots correctly to show which digits recur.

terms.definition: A formula that defines each term of a sequence using the previous term(s).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The recursive formula u(n+1) = 2u(n) + 1 with u(1) = 3 gives 3, 7, 15, 31...
terms.common_errors: Confusing recursive (needs previous term) with explicit (calculates directly from n).

terms.definition: A sample that accurately reflects the characteristics of the whole population.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: To be representative, the sample should include students from all year groups.
terms.common_errors: Selecting a convenient but unrepresentative group.

terms.definition: The reduced set of outcomes when a condition is applied, used in conditional probability.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Given that the dice shows an even number, the restricted sample space is {2, 4, 6}.
terms.common_errors: Using the full sample space instead of the restricted one.

terms.definition: The single vector that has the same effect as two or more vectors combined.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the resultant of vectors a and b by adding them: a + b.

terms.definition: A value of x that makes an equation equal to zero; also the inverse of a power (e.g. square root).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The roots of x² - 5x + 6 = 0 are x = 2 and x = 3.
terms.common_errors: Confusing roots of an equation with square roots.

terms.definition: A subset of a population selected for study, used to make inferences about the whole group.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: We surveyed a sample of 50 students from the school.
terms.common_errors: Making the sample too small or not representative.

terms.definition: A quantity that has magnitude only (no direction), as opposed to a vector.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Speed is a scalar; velocity is a vector.
terms.common_errors: Confusing scalar (magnitude only) with vector (magnitude and direction).

terms.definition: A formal way of describing collections of numbers using curly brackets, ∈, ∪, ∩, and other symbols.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A = {x : x > 3} means the set of all values greater than 3.
terms.common_errors: Confusing ∪ (union, 'or') with ∩ (intersection, 'and').

terms.definition: The number of meaningful digits in a value, starting from the first non-zero digit.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Round 0.03456 to 2 significant figures: 0.035.
terms.common_errors: Confusing significant figures with decimal places.

terms.definition: A formula for any triangle: a/sinA = b/sinB = c/sinC, used when you know an angle and its opposite side.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the sine rule when you know a side and the angle opposite to it.
terms.common_errors: Using the sine rule when the cosine rule is needed (SAS problems).

terms.definition: The distance measured along the sloping surface of a cone or pyramid from base to apex.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The slant height of this cone is 13 cm; use it to find the curved surface area.
terms.common_errors: Confusing slant height with perpendicular height.

terms.definition: The complete set of all values that satisfy an equation or inequality.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The solution set of x² < 9 is {x : -3 < x < 3}.
terms.common_errors: Giving only one solution when there are two (e.g. for quadratics).

terms.definition: The values of unknowns that make an equation or system of equations true.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find both solutions to x² - 7x + 10 = 0.
terms.common_errors: Stopping at one solution for a quadratic.

terms.definition: Distinct subgroups within a population (e.g. year groups, genders) used in stratified sampling.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Divide the school population into strata by year group.
terms.common_errors: Creating strata that overlap.

terms.definition: A sampling method where the population is divided into strata and a proportional number is randomly selected from each.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use stratified sampling: if Year 7 is 20% of the school, 20% of your sample should be Year 7.
terms.common_errors: Not making the sample proportional to the strata sizes.

terms.definition: A transformation that changes the size of a shape in one direction only, either horizontally or vertically.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: y = 2f(x) is a vertical stretch of f(x) by scale factor 2.
terms.common_errors: Confusing stretches with enlargements (which scale in all directions).

terms.definition: An inequality using < or > (not ≤ or ≥), where the boundary value is not included.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: x > 3 is a strict inequality — x can be 3.001 but not exactly 3.
terms.common_errors: Using a solid line or filled circle when it should be dashed/open for strict inequalities.

terms.definition: To create or span an angle from a particular point or arc.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The angle subtended at the centre by this arc is twice the angle subtended at the circumference.

terms.definition: Listing all possible outcomes or combinations in an organised way to ensure none are missed.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use systematic enumeration to list all possible three-digit codes using digits 1, 2, 3.
terms.common_errors: Jumping around instead of following a systematic pattern.

terms.definition: A sampling method where items are selected at regular intervals from an ordered list (e.g. every 10th person).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Choose every 5th name from the register — this is systematic sampling.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with random sampling — systematic has a fixed interval.

terms.definition: A mathematical statement that has been formally proved to be true.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: From Greek 'theorema' (speculation, proposition to be proved)
terms.example_usage: Pythagoras' theorem states that a² + b² = c² in a right-angled triangle.

terms.definition: Relating to the study of relationships between angles and sides of triangles using sine, cosine, and tangent.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use trigonometric ratios to find the missing angle.

terms.definition: A ratio simplified so that one of the quantities is 1, often written as 1:n or n:1.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Simplify 5:8 to a unit ratio: 1:1.6.
terms.common_errors: Not dividing both parts by the same value.

terms.definition: The highest value that can belong to a class interval in grouped data, used when plotting cumulative frequency.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: For the class 10-20, the upper class boundary is 20.
terms.common_errors: Using the midpoint instead of the upper boundary for cumulative frequency plots.

terms.definition: A proportional relationship where one quantity increases at the same rate as another: y = kx.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If y varies directly with x, doubling x doubles y.
terms.common_errors: Confusing direct variation with inverse variation.

terms.definition: A relationship where one quantity increases as the other decreases proportionally: y = k/x.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If y varies inversely with x, doubling x halves y.
terms.common_errors: Writing y = kx instead of y = k/x for inverse variation.

terms.definition: A probability experiment where each selected item is returned before the next selection, keeping probabilities constant.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Drawing with replacement: pick a ball, note its colour, put it back, then pick again.
terms.common_errors: Treating without-replacement problems as with-replacement.

terms.definition: A probability experiment where selected items are not returned, changing the probabilities for subsequent selections.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Drawing without replacement: the second probability depends on what was drawn first.
terms.common_errors: Not adjusting the denominator for subsequent picks.
